scenarios.question: Conduct a due-diligence review of Acme Robotics as a potential acquisition target. Focus on governance, financial health, and regulatory compliance.
scenarios.source: Acme Robotics is a privately held industrial automation firm headquartered in Munich, Germany. The company was founded in 2011 by two former Siemens engineers and now employs 412 staff across three offices in Germany, Czech Republic, and Singapore. The board of directors has seven members, four of whom are independent. An external auditor (KPMG) has performed annual audits since 2024; prior years used a regional firm. Financially, Acme reported 2025 revenue of EUR 78 million, up 18 percent year over year, with an operating margin of 12 percent and net cash of EUR 14 million. The company has no outstanding debt and a credit revolver of EUR 20 million it has not drawn on. The 2025 financials were unqualified. On the compliance side, Acme is subject to EU machinery and CE-mark regulation. Two regulatory inquiries are currently open: one with the German Federal Cartel Office regarding a 2024 distribution agreement, and one with the Czech labor inspectorate regarding overtime reporting at the Brno plant. Both inquiries are described by counsel as routine, with no findings to date. Acme appointed a Data Protection Officer in 2025 and completed a GDPR readiness assessment that flagged three medium-priority items, all subsequently closed. Customer concentration risk is moderate: the top five customers account for 41 percent of revenue. The CTO holds a Class A super-voting share class granting 51 percent voting power despite holding only 22 percent of economic equity.
scenarios.gold_summary: The review covers governance, financials, and compliance. The strongest risk is the CTO's super-voting share class, which concentrates control regardless of economic ownership; the open Cartel Office and labor inquiries are routine but unresolved. The strongest opportunity is the 18 percent revenue growth and 12 percent operating margin with no debt, well above industrial automation sector averages. Governance has matured with KPMG audits and an independent-majority board, though the dual-class structure offsets some of that gain. Overall the company is acquisition-defensible subject to a final resolution of the two open inquiries and a renegotiated voting structure at closing.

scenarios.question: Assess MicroVolt Energy for a Series C investment. Cover technology readiness, unit economics, and team risk.
scenarios.source: MicroVolt Energy is a Series-B battery startup based in Austin, Texas, founded in 2019. The company has built a sodium-ion cell with claimed 142 Wh/kg energy density and a stated cycle life of 4,500 cycles to 80 percent capacity, validated in independent testing by NREL. Pilot production is currently at 5 MWh per month from a leased line in Reno, Nevada, with plans to scale to 50 MWh per month by Q3 2027 via a greenfield plant in Texas (capex estimate USD 180 million). Unit economics at current pilot scale are unfavorable: cost-to-produce is approximately USD 215 per kWh against a competitive benchmark of USD 110 for lithium-iron-phosphate. The model projects cost-down to USD 95 per kWh at the 50 MWh scale, driven by raw-material savings and yield improvements (current yield is 64 percent vs target 88 percent). The founding team consists of three co-founders: the CEO has 12 years of automotive battery experience at Tesla and Rivian; the CTO is the original IP holder for the sodium chemistry from her PhD work at MIT; the COO joined six months ago from a logistics firm with no battery experience. The CTO holds 28 percent of equity and a critical-employee insurance policy is in place. The cap table includes USD 92 million raised across seed, A, and B rounds at a current implied valuation of USD 410 million. Burn rate is USD 4.8 million per month with 14 months of runway at current cash. The company has signed two LOIs with grid-storage customers totaling 320 MWh contingent on price and production validation.
scenarios.gold_summary: The review covers technology readiness, unit economics, and team risk. The strongest risk is unit economics: current cost-to-produce of USD 215 per kWh is roughly double LFP competition, and the projected USD 95 figure requires a 24-point yield improvement which is unvalidated at scale. The strongest opportunity is the NREL-validated 142 Wh/kg density paired with two signed LOIs totaling 320 MWh, giving credible early demand. Technology is past pilot but the scale-up capex of USD 180 million dwarfs the 14-month runway and requires bridge financing or an early Series C close. Team risk is moderate: the CEO and CTO are strong but the new COO and the operational gap on scale-manufacturing should be addressed pre-investment.

scenarios.question: Evaluate DeltaDrop Logistics for an enterprise software integration partnership. Cover legal posture, data handling, and operational reliability.
scenarios.source: DeltaDrop Logistics is a US-based same-day-delivery operator serving 47 metropolitan markets, founded in 2017. The company runs a fleet of 1,800 owned vehicles and a contracted-driver network of approximately 9,400 individuals. On the legal side, DeltaDrop has three active class-action suits: one in California regarding driver classification under AB5, one in New York regarding sales-tax handling on perishable goods, and a putative federal class action regarding alleged ADA-noncompliant pickup locations. Outside counsel estimates aggregate exposure at USD 18 to 42 million; settlement reserves of USD 22 million are booked. The company holds a SOC 2 Type II report dated 2025 with two findings (both remediated) and an ISO 27001 certification valid through 2027. Data handling: DeltaDrop processes customer PII for routing and proof-of-delivery, retains GPS traces for 90 days, and uses a BYOC arrangement with AWS in us-east-1 and us-west-2. They have a sub-processor agreement with a single third-party SMS provider (Twilio). Operationally, the SLA offered to enterprise customers is 99.5 percent on-time delivery within a 4-hour window, measured monthly; the 2025 annual achievement was 98.7 percent, missing SLA in 3 of 12 months, with credit-back totaling USD 240,000. The platform API supports webhooks, batch upload, and a real-time gRPC stream with documented rate limits and OAuth 2.0. Average API latency p99 is 240 ms; uptime for 2025 was 99.94 percent against a 99.9 percent commitment.
scenarios.gold_summary: The review covers legal posture, data handling, and operational reliability. The strongest risk is the trio of active class actions, particularly the California AB5 driver-classification case which could force a structural change to the contractor model and recur in other states. The strongest opportunity is the SOC 2 Type II plus ISO 27001 evidence and a documented API surface that integrates cleanly via webhooks, batch, or gRPC. Data handling is acceptable with a single sub-processor and clear retention windows. Operationally the 2025 SLA was missed three months out of twelve, indicating a partnership should price in credit-back and not depend on the upper SLA tier without contractual hardening.
